stories.headline: Israel kills Iran's security chief Ali Larijani in air strike near Tehran
stories.body: Israel confirmed that it killed Ali Larijani, the secretary of Iran's Supreme National Security Council and the most senior Iranian official assassinated since Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei was killed on the first day of US-Israeli strikes on February 28. Larijani's son, an aide, and bodyguards were also killed in the strike near Tehran on Monday night. The head of Iran's Basij paramilitary force, Gholamreza Soleimani, was confirmed dead in a separate attack. Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi told Al Jazeera that the killings would not destabilize Iran's political system, saying its institutions are robust and do not depend on any single individual. Iran's army leader vowed decisive retaliation, while Tehran mourned and promised revenge.

stories.headline: Trump lashes out at NATO allies for refusing to help secure Strait of Hormuz
stories.body: President Trump accused NATO members of making a foolish mistake by refusing to help the US secure the Strait of Hormuz during the war with Iran, while insisting the US does not need their assistance. Speaking alongside Irish PM Micheal Martin at the White House, Trump reserved his harshest criticism for the UK, saying the US-UK relationship was the best until Keir Starmer came along. France's Macron said France would never take part in operations to reopen the strait, while EU top diplomat Kaja Kallas said it was not Europe's war. Only a handful of vessels carrying Iranian oil have managed to pass through the strait since hostilities began, severely disrupting global energy supplies. Trump also confirmed delaying his planned meeting with China's Xi Jinping from late March to late April, saying he needs to remain in Washington due to the war.

stories.headline: Top US counterterrorism official Joe Kent resigns over Iran war
stories.body: Joe Kent, the director of the US National Counterterrorism Center, resigned and publicly criticized the war on Iran, saying in a letter posted on social media that Iran posed no imminent threat to the US and accusing the administration of starting the war due to pressure from Israel and its powerful American lobby. Kent, a US special forces and CIA veteran whose wife was killed in a bombing in Syria, said he could not support sending the next generation to fight in a war that serves no benefit to the American people. The Anti-Defamation League accused his resignation letter of trafficking in antisemitic tropes. Trump dismissed Kent as weak on security and said the resignation made him realize it was good that Kent was out.

stories.headline: Pakistan air strike on Kabul rehab centre kills over 400
stories.body: A Pakistani air strike hit a state-run drug rehabilitation hospital in Kabul on Monday evening, killing more than 400 people and injuring 265 in the deadliest single attack of the three-week war between the two countries. Survivors described horrific scenes of patients burning in their beds or being crushed by collapsing walls. The Norwegian Refugee Council confirmed seeing hundreds of casualties. The UN called for an independent investigation. The conflict, which escalated in February when Pakistan launched strikes on militant targets in Afghanistan, has been overshadowed internationally by the US-Israeli war on Iran.

stories.headline: Cuba's power grid collapses again as US oil blockade tightens
stories.body: Cuba's national electric grid collapsed on Monday, leaving approximately 10 million people without power amid a US-imposed oil blockade that has crippled the island's already obsolete generation system. It is the sixth nationwide blackout in 18 months. Cuba has received only two small oil shipments all year, with Venezuela, its main supplier, sending no fuel since the US captured President Maduro in January. Trump said he thought he would have the honor of taking Cuba, describing it as a very weakened nation. The crisis last weekend sparked rare violent protests on the island.

stories.headline: Growing Republican rift over Iran war as Kent resignation shakes MAGA base
stories.body: The resignation of counterterrorism chief Joe Kent has exposed a widening divide among Republicans over the war in Iran and US support for Israel. Kent, a decorated veteran and longtime Trump supporter, accused the administration of being manipulated into the conflict by Israeli officials and pro-Israel lobbying groups, reflecting a growing isolationist strain within the MAGA movement. A marine veteran charged for a violent anti-war protest in Washington told media that Trump is wrong on the Iran war. Top US intelligence officials are set to testify before Congress about the conflict and threats to the homeland, as the war enters its third week with no clear end in sight.

stories.headline: Extreme weather batters US from coast to coast
stories.body: More than half the US population faced extreme weather conditions on Monday, with a prolonged and unusual March heat wave gripping cities across the West with triple-digit temperatures, blizzards burying parts of the Midwest, and storms rolling into the East Coast. Flight cancellations and delays continued across the country as snow dumped on the Midwest and severe weather moved eastward. Hazardous road conditions were issued across Minnesota, Michigan, and Wisconsin, while California faced record-breaking heat. The wild March weather has caused power outages, property damage, and at least one death.

stories.headline: Illinois primaries: Juliana Stratton wins Democratic Senate nomination
stories.body: Illinois Lieutenant Governor Juliana Stratton won the Democratic primary for US Senate on Tuesday, edging out two sitting members of the House to advance to the November general election against Republican nominee Don Tracy, the former state party chair. In other results, Daniel Biss, mayor of Evanston, won the Democratic nomination for the 9th congressional district, while centrist Melissa Bean won the 8th district primary. On the Republican side, Darren Bailey won a second chance to challenge Governor JB Pritzker.

stories.headline: Seven-ton meteor lights up the sky over Cleveland
stories.body: A meteor weighing seven tons and nearly six feet in diameter sped across the Cleveland sky at 45,000 miles per hour on Tuesday morning before breaking apart in a thunderous boom that shook homes and startled residents across multiple states. The American Meteor Society received reports from Wisconsin to Maryland, and NASA confirmed it was first spotted about 50 miles above Lake Erie before fragmenting over Valley City, north of Medina, releasing energy equivalent to 250 tons of TNT. No debris was immediately found on the ground.

stories.headline: Postmaster General warns USPS will run out of cash within a year
stories.body: Postmaster General Louis DeJoy warned Congress that the US Postal Service will be out of cash in under a year, highlighting the dire financial state of the agency. The warning comes as the postal service continues to grapple with declining mail volumes, rising costs, and the financial burden of pre-funding retiree benefits. The USPS has been struggling to modernize its operations while maintaining universal service, and the cash crunch threatens to force drastic cuts to operations if Congress does not intervene.

stories.headline: King Felipe acknowledges colonial abuses in Mexico, sparking fierce political debate
stories.body: King Felipe VI became the first Spanish monarch to publicly acknowledge abuses during Spain's colonization of the Americas, saying there had been a lot of abuse during the conquest and that some aspects of the history cannot make Spaniards feel proud. Mexico's President Claudia Sheinbaum welcomed the comments as a gesture of reconciliation. The Spanish government endorsed the king's words fully, while PP leader Feijoo called historical revisionism a disparate and urged context. Vox praised Spain's colonial legacy as the greatest civilizing work in history. The remarks reignited the long-running debate about Spain's colonial past and were seen as an attempt to rescue the upcoming Ibero-American summit.

stories.headline: Feijoo presses Vox to close coalition deals after Castilla y Leon elections
stories.body: PP leader Alberto Nunez Feijoo urged Vox leader Santiago Abascal to finalize coalition agreements in Extremadura, Aragon, and Castilla y Leon before April, saying the electoral cycle that began in December is now closed and it would be undemocratic for Vox to block PP-led governments. Feijoo met with the three regional PP candidates and confirmed that Extremadura is closest to a deal, with a programmatic agreement practically finalized. The PP has given its regional leaders the green light to offer Vox cabinet positions. However, Abascal refused to guarantee four-year stability pacts, raising concerns about a repeat of the last coalition's abrupt end.

stories.headline: Vox's growth stalls below 20% in Castilla y Leon as analysts debate the far right's ceiling
stories.body: Vox grew in the Castilla y Leon elections but fell short of the 20% threshold it had been targeting, prompting debate over whether the far-right party has hit a ceiling. Analysts point to several factors for the underwhelming result: a useful vote shift toward the PP among older voters wary of instability, backlash against Vox's alignment with Trump during an unpopular war, internal party turmoil including the expulsion of former secretary general Ortega Smith, and memories of the previous Vox-PP coalition government. Former Vox officials including Espinosa and Ortega Smith called for an extraordinary party congress, deepening internal tensions.

stories.headline: PSC and ERC race to save Catalan budget as IRPF dispute remains unresolved
stories.body: With just three days until the vote on amendments to reject the Catalan budget outright, the PSC-led government and ERC are holding intensified meetings to find a way forward, but the central government in Madrid has declined to make the gesture ERC demands: a clear commitment to transfer management of income tax (IRPF) to the Generalitat. ERC has said this transfer is a non-negotiable condition from the investiture pact that brought Salvador Illa to power. The Catalan government has tried to pressure ERC by citing the economic consequences of the Iran war and the Generalitat's looming cash shortage, but ERC considers opportunities slipping away with no concrete commitment from Madrid.

stories.headline: Barcelona gives final approval to tram connection across the Diagonal
stories.body: Barcelona's city council granted definitive approval on Tuesday to reform the Avinguda Diagonal between Passeig de Sant Joan and Placa Francesc Macia to complete the connection of the city's two separate tram networks. The project, which will take an estimated 40 months, was supported by PSC, Barcelona en Comu, and ERC, while Junts, PP, and Vox voted against. The total investment is estimated at 53 million euros for the street redesign plus 62 million for a new storm drain, with the Generalitat expected to contribute 80 million euros. Barcelona en Comu criticized the government for waiting three years into its mandate to approve the project.

stories.headline: Fire in tunnel disrupts Rodalies commuter rail across Barcelona
stories.body: A fire in the cabling between Barcelona Sants and Placa Catalunya stations on Tuesday afternoon caused major disruptions to the commuter rail network, cutting service on lines R1 and R4 through Barcelona's central tunnel. R1 trains terminated at Badalona, while R4 services were rerouted via Passeig de Gracia, skipping five central Barcelona stops including Arc de Triomf and Placa Catalunya. The fire was extinguished before 7:30 PM and technicians worked to repair the damaged cables, with Renfe advising passengers to check service status on Wednesday morning before heading to stations.

stories.headline: Teachers' strike continues across Catalunya with protests and sit-ins
stories.body: The second day of rolling teachers' strikes in Catalunya focused on the Penedes, Tarragona, and Terres de l'Ebre regions, with an estimated 38% of called workers participating according to the Education Department. Around 4,000 people marched in Tarragona, and teachers staged sit-ins at education department offices in Manresa and at an institute in Barcelona's Barri Besos neighborhood. The major union Ustec called the day a resounding success and demanded the government return to the negotiating table this Thursday. The strikes, organized by unions Ustec, Aspepc, and CGT, oppose a recent salary agreement between the Education Department and the unions CCOO and UGT.

stories.headline: Barcelona approves Thyssen Museum plan for former Comedia cinema
stories.body: Barcelona's urban planning commission approved the provisional modification to allow the future Thyssen Museum to be installed in the Palau Marcet, the former Comedia cinema at the corner of Passeig de Gracia and Gran Via. The plan, backed by PSC, Junts, PP, and Vox, will transform the heritage building into a cultural venue with exhibition spaces, a 450-seat auditorium, a restricted-access restaurant, and a specialized shop. Two existing clothing stores in the building have until December 2029 to vacate. Barcelona en Comu and ERC voted against, warning the museum could attract an additional million visitors to the already saturated Passeig de Gracia area.

stories.headline: Hiker killed by avalanche in the Catalan Pyrenees
stories.body: A 37-year-old hiker died after being caught by an avalanche in the Pallars Sobira area of the Catalan Pyrenees. In lighter mountain news, skiers at the Tavascan resort filmed a mother bear and two cubs running across the slopes after emerging from hibernation, a rare and delightful sighting in the region.
